result:
The rain doesn’t fall here the way it does on Earth. It arrives in slow, deliberate sheets that hang in the air like silver curtains before deciding to descend. I watch it from the porch of a house that technically doesn’t exist anymore, sipping something that tastes like childhood memories and burnt sugar. The cat on my lap (who may or may not be a ghost) purrs in a frequency that makes the flowers open their petals wider, revealing tiny galaxies inside.

Sometimes I wonder if I’ve been writing the same story for twenty years without realizing it. Every character I create seems to carry the same quiet grief wrapped in sarcasm, the same habit of looking at the moon like it owes them answers. Maybe that’s okay. Maybe art is just finding new costumes for the same nervous heart.

There’s a particular kind of freedom in being slightly irrelevant. When no one’s watching your next move too closely, you can dance badly in the kitchen at 3 a.m. to songs that haven’t been popular since 2009. You can admit that you still don’t understand taxes or how pineapples grow. You can say the word “ineffable” out loud just because it feels good in your mouth.

The rain has stopped now. The cat (definitely a ghost) has dissolved into fireflies. I remain, 250 words older, still unsure about most things but increasingly certain that tenderness is a radical act in a world that keeps trying to sell us armor.

Tomorrow I’ll probably write another story about lonely people looking at celestial bodies. And that, I’ve decided, is a perfectly reasonable way to spend a life.

Maybe art is just finding new costumes for the same nervous heart.

There’s a particular kind of freedom in being slightly irrelevant. When no one’s watching your next move too closely, you can dance badly in the kitchen at 3 a.m. to songs that haven’t been popular since 2009. You can admit that you still don’t understand taxes or how pineapples grow. You can say the word “ineffable” out loud just because it feels good in your mouth.

The rain has stopped now. The cat (definitely a ghost) has dissolved into fireflies. I remain, 250 words older, still unsure about most things but increasingly certain that tenderness is a radical act in a world that keeps trying to sell us armor.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
